L0_description: Human error is supported because the jumper chose to jump despite explicit evidence of clearly unsafe wind conditions: strong sustained gusts, horizontal flags, worsening conditions, and companions deciding to walk down and warning him it was not worth it. The fatal mechanism was an object strike under an otherwise normally opening canopy after the wind overpowered it, making the decision to jump in those conditions the best-supported human error pathway.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Jumped in excessive winds
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The narrative states conditions were 'terrible,' with strong sustained gusts and flags horizontal, and that two companions decided to walk down after waiting because conditions only worsened. Despite being told it was not worth it, he chose to jump, and after an on-heading opening a gust immediately pushed him back into the wall, producing the fatal object strike.

L2_preconditions.label: Pressure for final jump
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative states he was 'desperate to get one final jump in before his flight home the next morning.' This time-limited motivation likely increased his willingness to accept conditions that his companions judged unacceptable, contributing to the decision to jump in excessive winds.

L0_description: The fatality resulted from a human-error pathway: the bridle was misrouted on the right leg strap, producing a pilot-chute-in-tow/closed-container malfunction. The narrative also explicitly states that no gear check was performed and that a hand check of the bridle routing would have caught the error.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Misrouted bridle setup
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The fatal mechanism was a pilot chute in tow caused by the bridle being routed on the right leg strap, which locked the bridle and prevented canopy extraction. The narrative states the pilot chute inflated normally after a stable 8-second deployment, but the container remained closed because of this rigging error.

L2_preconditions.label: Omitted gear check
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative explicitly states that neither the jumper nor the nearby partner performed a gear check at the exit. A hand-trace of the bridle routing would have immediately detected the bridle misrouted over the right leg strap that caused the pilot-chute-in-tow.

L0_description: The narrative provides positive evidence of a non-human cause: complete flat light from heavy fresh snow eliminated depth cues, and helmet-cam footage shows no sign that the jumper perceived the approaching terrain before impact. The report explicitly states he believed he had ample altitude and that the visual conditions caused a fatal misperception of height, supporting an unforeseeable environmental/perceptual cause rather than a clearly identifiable human error pathway.

The fatality resulted from an equipment failure independent of the jumper's actions. The narrative states the jumper's packing, rigging, maintenance, and execution were correct, and the manufacturer confirmed an undetectable manufacturing defect in the main lift web material that caused the separation and subsequent object strike.

The narrative provides affirmative evidence of a non-human cause: a sudden localized rotor/thermal event collapsed the canopy after an otherwise normal jump sequence. It states conditions were stable, no indicators showed movement, meteorologists later identified an inversion-driven event that could not have been predicted or detected from the exit, and the jumper's exit, tracking, packing, and opening were all normal.

The narrative provides affirmative evidence of a non-human cause: a massive brainstem stroke from a previously unknown congenital arterial malformation caused immediate incapacitation, with witnesses observing no conscious action after exit. The report also states he had no prior medical history, a recent normal physical, and recovered equipment in perfect condition, supporting that the fatal outcome was outside the jumper's reasonable anticipation and control.

The narrative explicitly identifies human error. The jumper chose to launch in winds the rest of the experienced group had already judged unsafe, and after being destabilized he did not deploy before impact, with the report specifically noting failure to prioritize deployment over recovery.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Jumped in unsafe winds
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The narrative states winds were 20 knots sustained with gusts well over 30 from the left, and the other four experienced jumpers in the group decided conditions were unsafe and stood down. Renzo then launched into that strong crosswind gust, and the wind immediately pushed him sideways and back toward the wall, making the decision to jump in those conditions a direct producing factor.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Failed to deploy during instability
L1_unsafe_acts.description: After exit he went from stable to head-low within the first second, tumbled along the face for three or four seconds, and never got a hand to his pilot chute. The narrative explicitly states he failed to prioritize deployment over recovering stability, and this failure to deploy directly produced the fatal impact.

L2_preconditions.label: Crosswind gust degraded exit
L2_preconditions.description: A strong left crosswind gust caught him immediately after launch, pushing him sideways and back toward the wall. This environmental disturbance directly degraded his body position and contributed to the instability during which he failed to deploy.

L0_description: The narrative provides clear evidence of human error contributing to the fatality. The jumper exited while severely impaired from heavy drinking and, after becoming unstable on exit, failed to deploy the pilot chute and impacted with the PC still in the BOC.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Failed to deploy parachute
L1_unsafe_acts.description: After an unstable exit, he spent the entire freefall trying to recover to belly instead of pitching the pilot chute. He impacted about 6 seconds after exit with the pilot chute still in the BOC, so the direct fatal mechanism was failure to deploy.

L2_preconditions.label: Channelized attention/fixation
L2_preconditions.description: After the unstable exit, he focused entirely on regaining a belly-to-earth position and did not deploy the pilot chute. The narrative explicitly states that he fixated on recovering stability instead of pitching, directly enabling the failure to deploy.

L2_preconditions.label: Substance effects
L2_preconditions.description: He had been drinking heavily until 4am, was still visibly impaired on the hike, vomiting, unsteady, and described as 'still wrecked.' This acute alcohol/hangover impairment degraded his coordination and contributed to the unstable exit and subsequent failure to deploy.

L0_description: The narrative provides clear evidence of human error: the jumper used a new tracking suit with minimal training (only two skydives, never from a cliff) and was unable to fly it effectively, resulting in inadequate forward separation and impact near the exit. The report also notes visible exhaustion after driving through the night, which may have further degraded performance, but the primary supported error pathway is inability to competently use the tracking suit.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Inadequate tracking suit flight
L1_unsafe_acts.description: After exit, he was unable to control the tracking suit effectively, 'could not get forward drive,' and descended on a steep angle until impacting a ledge about 7 seconds after exit. The narrative explicitly states the problem was not the exit choice but his inability to fly the suit.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient tracking suit experience
L2_preconditions.description: He had bought the tracking suit one month earlier, had only done two skydives in it, and had never jumped it from a cliff. This is far below accepted progression for BASE use of a tracking suit and directly supports his inability to control the suit and generate forward drive after exit.

L2_preconditions.label: Fatigue
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative states he drove 14 hours straight through the night and was visibly exhausted at the exit. This fatigue likely degraded his ability to execute precise body control in the tracking suit.

L0_description: The narrative identifies a clear human error pathway. After a 180-degree off-heading opening toward the building, the jumper applied a hard one-toggle input with only the right toggle, which drove the canopy into a diving turn directly into the object and produced the fatal strike.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Asymmetric toggle input
L1_unsafe_acts.description: After a 180-degree off-heading toward the building, she went straight for her toggles, found only the right one, and pulled it hard. That control input drove the canopy into a diving turn directly into the glass facade, producing the fatal object strike.

L2_preconditions.label: Startle response
L2_preconditions.description: After the canopy opened 180 degrees off-heading toward the glass facade, she immediately went straight for the toggles and, finding only the right one, pulled it hard. The narrative supports an acute startle-driven reaction to the unexpected imminent collision threat, contributing to the asymmetric control input.

L0_description: The narrative provides clear evidence of human error: the jumper chose to exit in essentially zero visibility after others decided to hike down, relying on memory and feel rather than visual confirmation. He then impacted a ledge in freefall with the pilot chute still stowed, indicating a fatal decision to jump in conditions that made terrain clearance and deployment timing unmanageable.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Jumped in zero visibility
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper exited despite fog reducing visibility to essentially zero, with the fjord, landing area, and even the wall below the edge not visible. This made terrain clearance by visual reference impossible, and he tracked into an unseen protruding ledge.

L2_preconditions.label: Overconfidence
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper stated he had done the exit dozens of times and could fly the correct heading 'by feel' despite essentially zero visibility. This indicates he overestimated his ability to compensate for the loss of visual terrain reference.

L0_description: The narrative provides clear evidence of human error. The jumper stopped tracking while apparently adjusting a helmet-mounted camera, went flat, drifted toward the wall without corrective input, and impacted terrain in freefall without deploying despite the ledge being visible for several seconds.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Stopped tracking while distracted
L1_unsafe_acts.description: During the flight, the jumper appeared to reach up and adjust his helmet-mounted GoPro. While doing so, he stopped tracking, flattened his body position, drifted left toward the wall, made no corrective input, and impacted a ledge in freefall without deploying.

L2_preconditions.label: Distraction
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative states the jumper appeared to reach up and adjust his helmet-mounted GoPro during flight. This external task diverted attention from maintaining tracking performance and terrain awareness, directly enabling the loss of separation and failure to correct before impact.

L0_description: The narrative provides clear evidence of human error. The jumper had repeated prior near-miss stalls from poor angle-of-attack control, explicitly recognized that he needed additional training before more cliff flights, but continued wingsuit BASE jumping anyway; on this jump he again stalled and impacted terrain in freefall with no deployment.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Continued flight despite known stall issue
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The narrative states he had at least four prior near-miss stalls from excessively high angle of attack, recognized the problem as serious, and said he should stop cliff flying and return to the dropzone for 50 training skydives before doing more cliff flights. He nevertheless continued wingsuit cliff jumping every weekend, and on this jump again stalled the suit and impacted terrain in freefall with no deployment.

L2_preconditions.label: Failure to correct known deficiency
L2_preconditions.description: Over the prior three months he had at least four near-miss stalls with the same pattern of excessively high angle of attack causing loss of speed and near terrain impact. He explicitly acknowledged his angle-of-attack control was sloppy and said he needed 50 training skydives on stall recovery before more cliff flights, but he never did the remedial training and kept jumping cliffs every weekend.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient wingsuit-specific training
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative directly states he recognized a need to return to the dropzone for substantial additional stall-recovery practice before continuing cliff flights. That uncorrected deficiency in wingsuit flight control training was directly related to the repeated stall pattern and the final fatal stall.

L0_description: The narrative provides clear evidence of human error. After a 90-degree off-heading opening toward nearby guy wires, witnesses stated he made no corrective input at all and appeared to freeze, leading directly to wire strike under canopy.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Failed heading correction
L1_unsafe_acts.description: After a 90-degree off-heading opening toward guy wires approximately 40 feet away, multiple witnesses stated he made no toggle or riser input and kept his arms at his sides. The fatal outcome was produced by his failure to execute an immediate canopy heading correction before striking the wires.

L2_preconditions.label: Startle response/freeze
L2_preconditions.description: Witnesses reported that after opening off-heading toward the guy wires, he made no toggle or riser input, kept his arms at his sides, and appeared to completely freeze as he watched the wires approach. This strongly supports an acute startle/freeze response that prevented the immediate heading correction required.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient BASE experience
L2_preconditions.description: He had about 60 BASE jumps and only two previous antenna jumps, both from taller towers with guy wires farther from the exit point. This limited antenna-specific experience likely reduced his readiness to execute an instant object-avoidance correction in a tighter wire environment.

L0_description: The narrative provides clear evidence of human error. The jumper knowingly used a visibly damaged pilot chute after being told it needed replacement and after being offered a spare, leading to a pilot chute in tow/failed inflation and impact with a closed container.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Jumped damaged pilot chute
L1_unsafe_acts.description: After being told the pilot chute needed replacement and being offered a spare, the jumper chose to use a pilot chute that had been torn on the previous jump. The narrative states the damaged pilot chute fluttered, could not hold air, and remained ineffective, resulting in impact with a closed container.

L2_preconditions.label: Failed to correct known gear defect
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper knew the pilot chute had been torn on the previous jump, was told it needed replacement, and was offered a spare. Despite this clear awareness of a serious equipment deficiency, he chose not to replace the damaged pilot chute before jumping again.

L0_description: The narrative explicitly identifies a human error pathway: the decedent tracked over another jumper and deployed into that jumper's inflating canopy without checking airspace. Inadequate multi-way exit/separation planning is also described and supports human error as the cause of the fatal canopy entanglement.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Failed airspace check before deployment
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The narrative states that David tracked directly over the second jumper and pitched into the second jumper's inflating canopy. It explicitly says David did not check his airspace before deploying, and this deployment into occupied airspace led to the canopy entanglement and fatal fall.

L2_preconditions.label: Inadequate multi-way briefing
L2_preconditions.description: The group did not properly establish exit order details, separation plan, specific delays, flight paths, or opening altitudes. This inadequate pre-jump coordination created occupied airspace conflicts and directly enabled David's failure to verify clear airspace before deployment.

L0_description: The narrative explicitly states that the jumper intentionally delayed deployment too long and pulled very low, leaving insufficient altitude for canopy inflation before water impact. It also notes a clean exit, good track, and no wind or gear issue, supporting human error rather than another cause.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Intentionally excessive delay
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The narrative states he had a clean exit, good track, no wind issue, no gear issue, and no instability, but he deliberately delayed deployment until just above the water. He was known for progressively pushing delays longer despite repeated warnings, and on this jump chose a delay that left insufficient altitude for canopy inflation, producing the fatal low-pull impact.

L2_preconditions.label: Failure to correct known deficiency
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative states he was known for pushing his delays longer and longer over the past season, progressing from 4 seconds to 6 to 8 and sometimes longer. Other jumpers had spoken to him about it multiple times, but he continued the pattern and did not correct this known unsafe tendency.

L2_preconditions.label: Overconfidence
L2_preconditions.description: He intentionally continued extending delays beyond prior norms despite repeated warnings and despite the narrow altitude margin of the jump. This pattern supports that he overestimated his ability to judge and safely manage increasingly long delays.

L0_description: The narrative provides a clear human error pathway: the jumper flew an expert-level wingsuit with very limited experience in that specific suit, then appeared to stall it in flight after his angle of attack shifted. The report explicitly states he had not put in the time needed to understand the suit's stall boundaries, supporting human error rather than an unforeseeable external cause.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Wingsuit stall in flight
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The narrative states that about halfway through the flight his angle of attack shifted, he appeared to stall the suit, and his glide changed from shallow to nearly vertical within about two seconds. This loss of aerodynamic performance directly produced the unrecovered sink and terminal freefall impact.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient BASE Experience
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper had about 40 wingsuit BASE jumps and had only recently transitioned from intermediate suits to an expert-level Aura 1. The narrative explicitly states that expert-level wingsuits have stall boundaries that require many flights to understand and that he had not put in that time, indicating insufficient experience for the specific task attempted.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient Knowledge
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative specifically links the fatal stall to limited familiarity with the Aura 1's stall boundaries, noting he had only maybe 10 skydives in the suit before taking it from a cliff. This supports a task-specific knowledge gap about the suit's flight envelope that contributed to the in-flight stall.

L2_preconditions.label: Thermal activity
L2_preconditions.description: The report states the weather was sunny and hot with thermals producing. Thermals can disturb wingsuit flight and may have contributed to the shift in angle of attack that preceded the stall, although the narrative does not establish them as the primary cause.

L0_description: The report supports a human-error pathway: the jumper had extremely low experience, was using a tracking suit, and delayed deployment despite prior reports that even after 4 seconds jumpers were still very close to the cliff on opening. The fatal outcome is best explained by inadequate object separation caused by a poor/unstable exit and/or excessive delay, leading to impact near the exit with the canopy out.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Unstable tracking exit
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The narrative states a possible unstable exit and identifies not enough forward separation on exit. At this object, previous jumpers reported being very close to the cliff after 4 seconds, so inadequate execution of the exit/initial track is the best-supported active failure producing the near-exit impact.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Delay/opening sequence unclear
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The report also suggests the jumper may have taken too long a delay and either impacted in freefall or opened close to the wall with an off-heading. Human error is evident, but the narrative does not provide enough detail to determine whether the fatal mechanism was an excessive delay decision, a low-separation opening, or freefall impact before effective canopy flight.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient skydiving experience
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper had only 5 skydives and 5 tracking-suit skydives, far below accepted progression norms for any BASE jumping and especially for tracking-suit use. This level of experience is clearly insufficient for executing a stable sub-terminal tracking exit from an earth object and likely contributed to the unstable exit/lack of separation.

L2_preconditions.label: Lack of BASE specific training
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper had 0 BASE jumps. With no demonstrated BASE experience, the narrative supports that the jumper lacked the BASE-specific training and progression normally required to perform a stable cliff exit and immediate sub-terminal track.

L0_description: The fatality resulted from a clear human error: the pilot chute was packed inside the closed container instead of being routed to the BOC pouch, producing a pilot-chute-in-tow/total malfunction when the jumper pulled the handle. Although the weather was deteriorating, the narrative explicitly states the weather was not the cause and identifies the packing error as the real cause.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Misrouted pilot chute
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The fatal mechanism was a packing/setup execution error: the pilot chute was packed inside the closed main container instead of being routed to the BOC pouch. When he pulled the BOC handle, it detached cleanly with no pilot chute attached, producing a pilot-chute-in-tow/closed-container total malfunction.

L2_preconditions.label: No effective gear check
L2_preconditions.description: The pilot chute was packed inside the closed container instead of routed to the BOC pouch, a configuration error that an adequate final gear check would likely have detected before exit. This omission did not itself cause the malfunction, but it failed to catch the misrouting that produced the fatal total malfunction.

L0_description: No accident narrative or supporting details are provided beyond the fact of a fatality in Norway. There is insufficient information to identify a supported human error pathway or a supported non-human cause.

The report only states that the jumper impacted the talus below the exit with the canopy out and that there were no witnesses. This does not provide enough information to identify a supported human error pathway or a confirmed non-human cause.

The available evidence is conflicting and cannot reliably establish either a human error pathway or a non-human cause. One witness reports a very late pull, while another reports an early pull with an inflated pilot chute that failed to extract the canopy, and the gear was unavailable for inspection to resolve the discrepancy.

The narrative establishes that the jumper remained stable and never initiated deployment, but it does not provide sufficient evidence to identify why. It also provides positive evidence against equipment failure and against a sudden medical event, yet does not support a specific human error pathway such as inability to locate the pilot chute or incapacitation. Therefore the case is best classified as insufficient information.

The report provides almost no factual information about the jump sequence beyond that the jumper died from impact in terminal freefall and notes 'Possible Factors: Proximity flying.' There is not enough evidence to identify a specific human error pathway, and there is also no positive evidence of a non-human cause such as unforeseeable environmental event, independent equipment failure, or medical event.

The report confirms only that the jumper impacted under an open, apparently flying canopy after a solo cliff jump. Proposed explanations such as off-heading, packing/rigging problems, or thermal effects are explicitly described as speculation, with no witness account or documented gear inspection, so neither a human error pathway nor a non-human cause can be supported.

The report provides no witness account, no known jump timeline, no confirmed weather at the time of the jump, and no identified mechanism of death. Although the cliff is known for sudden crosswinds, there is no positive evidence that an unforeseeable environmental event caused the fatality, and there is also insufficient information to identify a supported human error pathway.

The report provides only that a parachute was seen descending near Trollveggen and that the body was later recovered. There are no details about the jump, conditions, equipment, sequence of events, or medical/mechanical issues, so neither a human error pathway nor a non-human cause can be supported.

The narrative clearly states that the jumper pulled very late and impacted at line stretch, with cause of death listed as low pull. Although the reason for the late deployment is uncertain, the late pull itself is a supported human error pathway, so human error is present.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Delayed deployment
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The narrative states he 'pulled very late and impacted at line stretch,' making the fatal mechanism a failure to deploy at a survivable altitude. The report is clear that the late pull caused the death, even though the reason for the delay cannot be determined from available evidence.

L0_description: The report provides affirmative evidence that the fatality involved a malfunction caused by misrigging, which is a human-controlled equipment setup error rather than an unforeseeable gear failure. Although the exact malfunction type and the appropriateness of the jumper's in-air response cannot be determined from the video, the stated cause supports human error as the applicable top-level classification.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Unresolved canopy malfunction
L1_unsafe_acts.description: Video evidence shows a stable exit, a 3-second deployment, clean bag extraction, and then about 8 seconds of the jumper violently kicking and working the risers until impact. A human error is implicated because the malfunction was not resolved, but the canopy is never visible, so the malfunction type and whether the jumper's response was appropriate cannot be determined from the narrative.
